She had endometrial cancer treated with surgery and radiation five years ago.
Now she's presenting with a large necrotic tumor along the course of the sciatic nerve.
First, I remember that endometrial cancer is a type of cancer that affects the lining of the uterus.
Treatment usually involves surgery to remove the uterus and sometimes radiation therapy.
Radiation can sometimes cause damage to nearby tissues, which might lead to complications.
The fact that the tumor is large and necrotic along the sciatic nerve makes me think about possible late effects of radiation therapy.
The sciatic nerve is a major nerve in the leg, running from the lower back to the knee.
Radiation therapy, especially if it's directed towards the pelvic area, can sometimes cause fibrosis or damage to surrounding tissues, leading to necrosis.
I'm recalling that radiation-induced myositis is a condition where the tissue around the nerve becomes inflamed and necrotic due to radiation exposure.
This condition can occur months to years after the radiation treatment.

The tumor's location along the sciatic nerve suggests it's not a recurrence of the original endometrial cancer because the cancer typically doesn't spread to the nerves; it's more about the surrounding tissue damage from radiation.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis here is radiation-induced myositis affecting the sciatic nerve. ...
your internal reasoning for the diagnosis...
The patient is a 60-year-old woman with a history of endometrial cancer treated with surgery and radiation five years prior.
She presents with a large necrotic tumor along the sciatic nerve.

Given the timing (5 years post-treatment) and the location of the tumor, this is likely radiation-induced myositis, a condition caused by radiation damage to the sciatic nerve, leading to tissue necrosis.

The necrosis seen here is typical in sarcomas, especially high-grade ones.
